summary: Kernel is memory-bound at 86.3% DRAM throughput with severe long scoreboard stalls from memory latency
reasoning: The kernel achieves 86.33% of peak DRAM bandwidth (2.11 TB/s out of 3.9 TB/s) while compute throughput is only 21.1%. The dominant stall reason is long scoreboard (55.86%), indicating warps are waiting on global memory loads. Low warp occupancy (30.03%) exacerbates the inability to hide memory latency. The L1 cache hit rate is very poor at 14.14%, meaning most accesses go to DRAM.

root_causes.cause: Poor L1 cache utilization with low hit rate causing excessive DRAM traffic

evidence.interpretation: Only 14% of L1 accesses hit cache, forcing 86% of requests to go to slower memory levels
DRAM is heavily utilized due to cache misses

root_causes.cause: Low occupancy prevents effective latency hiding for memory operations

evidence.interpretation: Only 30% warp occupancy means insufficient warps to hide memory latency
56% of stalls are waiting on global memory loads, directly tied to low occupancy

recommended_fixes.fix: Reduce register usage from 96 to under 64 registers per thread by using shared memory for intermediate channel values, enabling higher occupancy (currently limited to 5 blocks by registers)
recommended_fixes.rationale: With 96 registers per thread, occupancy is severely limited to ~5 blocks per SM. Reducing to 64 registers would allow 8+ blocks, increasing warp count to better hide the 55.86% long scoreboard stalls from memory latency
